answer:
## Trả lời thẳng: SwiftTerm thắng, chọn nó

Với kiến trúc của tool (host giữ PTY, stream raw VT byte stream qua NetBird/WireGuard, client chỉ render bytes + bắt phím gửi về), **SwiftTerm là lựa chọn đúng cho cả macOS và iOS/iPadOS ngay bây giờ.** Lý do bám sát 6 deciding factors:

1. **iOS support (REQUIRED)** — SwiftTerm coi iOS là first-class target: `Package.swift` khai báo `.iOS(.v14)`, có thư mục riêng `Sources/SwiftTerm/iOS/` với UIKit `iOSTerminalView` + SwiftUI `SwiftUITerminalView`, và đang chạy production trên App Store (La Terminal, Secure Shellfish) ([Package.swift](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/Package.swift), [README](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/README.md)). Ngược lại, libghostty-vt **không liệt kê iOS là build target được hỗ trợ** trong build system công khai; iOS chỉ khả thi qua XCFramework community.

2. **Embedding maturity** — SwiftTerm có `TerminalViewDelegate` là public contract ổn định, không có breaking change trong lịch sử commit 2025–2026, có DocC docs. libghostty-vt vẫn là **public alpha, chưa có tagged stable release tính đến tháng 6/2026** (đã verify — xem mục 3).

3. **Feed-bytes model** — đây là điểm khớp hoàn hảo. `Terminal.feed(byteArray:)` / `feed(buffer:)` nhận raw bytes từ bất kỳ nguồn nào (network socket), không cần local PTY. Phím gửi ngược qua `TerminalViewDelegate.send(source:data:)`. `HeadlessTerminal` bị guard `#if !os(iOS)` nhưng **không liên quan** — nó chỉ là wrapper PTY server-side; client iOS dùng `Terminal` + `TerminalView` trực tiếp ([Terminal.swift](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/Sources/SwiftTerm/Terminal.swift), [TerminalViewDelegate.swift](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/Sources/SwiftTerm/Apple/TerminalViewDelegate.swift)).

4. **Swift integration cost** — SwiftTerm là pure Swift/SPM, một dòng `.package(url:from:)`, zero bridging. libghostty cần C-ABI bridging + Zig toolchain (version pinning, iOS cross-compile không được hỗ trợ chính thức), hoặc phụ thuộc vào một XCFramework binary informal.

Hai factor còn lại (perf/correctness, maintenance/license) libghostty mạnh hơn về lý thuyết, nhưng không đủ để bù cho 4 factor trên ở thời điểm hiện tại.

## Bảng so sánh theo 6 yếu tố

| Yếu tố | SwiftTerm | libghostty (vt + GhosttyKit) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. iOS/iPadOS support** | First-class. `.iOS(.v14)`, thư mục `iOS/` riêng, ship production (La Terminal, Secure Shellfish). | **libghostty-vt KHÔNG list iOS** là build target chính thức. Khả thi qua `Lakr233/libghostty-spm` XCFramework (có iOS slice — đã verify). Các wrapper khác (briannadoubt/finnvoor GhosttyKit) **chỉ macOS** (đã verify). Team Ghostty: iOS là "community effort outside this repo". |
| **2. Embedding API maturity** | Mature, stable. `TerminalViewDelegate` không đổi breaking, DocC docs, 3+ App Store app dùng. | **Public alpha, KHÔNG có tagged release** tính đến 6/2026 (đã verify — refuted). Header `vt.h`: "not yet stable and is definitely going to change". Full `ghostty.h`: "isn't meant to be a general purpose embedding API (yet)". |
| **3. Feed-bytes / headless mode** | Khớp hoàn hảo. `feed(byteArray:)` không cần PTY; `delegate.send()` cho keystroke egress. | libghostty-vt khớp về mặt thiết kế (`ghostty_terminal_vt_write`), PTY-free. NHƯNG **full libghostty surface API giả định tự sở hữu PTY** (`command` field) — không có pipe-only mode documented. Dùng vt = phải tự viết renderer. |
| **4. Swift integration cost** | Thấp. Pure Swift/SPM, zero bridging. ~1–3 ngày tới prototype. | Medium-High nếu build từ Zig (version pinning + iOS cross-compile không hỗ trợ). Medium nếu dùng XCFramework binary (opaque, version informal). |
| **5. Render perf + correctness** | CoreText/CoreGraphics (stable default) + Metal opt-in (mới, 3/2026, đang vá). Kitty keyboard/graphics, Sixel, Unicode. Ligatures: **không có switch tắt** — dùng font không-ligature là xong (đã verify). | **Industry-leading.** Metal GPU pipeline Ghostty-class, HarfBuzz shaping, Kitty graphics/keyboard, SIMD parsing. Đây là điểm mạnh thật của libghostty. |
| **6. Maintenance + license** | MIT. Rất active: 317 commits 2025–2026, last commit 18/5/2026, nhiều contributor (không bus-factor). | MIT. Ghostty active mạnh, nhưng iOS path phụ thuộc maintainer cộng đồng (Lakr233) auto-publish theo Ghostty tag. |

## Điểm mạnh thật của libghostty — và vì sao chưa phù hợp

**Mạnh thật (không phủ nhận):**
- **Rendering correctness là tốt nhất trong các terminal hiện tại** — Metal GPU pipeline, HarfBuzz shaping đầy đủ, Kitty graphics protocol, SIMD VT parsing. Cho daily coding text-heavy thì chất lượng render của Ghostty là chuẩn vàng ([libghostty-is-coming](https://mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming)).
- **libghostty-vt đúng về mặt kiến trúc** cho remote-bytes: headless, PTY-free, `ghostty_terminal_vt_write` + render-state API tách bạch parsing/state khỏi rendering/transport.
- **Có app iOS thật đang ship**: VVTerm (App ID 6757482822, đã verify live, iOS 16.1+/iPadOS/macOS/visionOS, v2.2), Echo, Geistty, Spectty — chứng minh path khả thi.

**Vì sao chưa phù hợp (decision-critical, đã verify):**
1. **Chưa có tagged stable release** (refuted claim ngược lại). Ghostty 1.3.0 release notes (9/3/2026) nói nguyên văn: *"We aren't ready to tag a versioned release for either of these modules yet"* và *"We aren't sure yet when we'll tag the first libghostty releases"* ([1.3.0 notes](https://ghostty.org/docs/install/release-notes/1-3-0)). Mục tiêu "~6 tháng" của Mitchell (≈3/2026) đã **không đạt** và vốn là conditional.
2. **iOS không phải first-class build target** cho libghostty-vt public. Phải đi qua `Lakr233/libghostty-spm` XCFramework community — chọn nhầm wrapper (briannadoubt/finnvoor) sẽ **link error trên iOS** vì chỉ có macOS-arm64 slice (đã verify).
3. **Lựa chọn khó chịu giữa vt và full lib:** libghostty-vt cho state nhưng **bạn phải tự viết Metal/SwiftUI renderer** (mất hết lợi thế render Ghostty-class trừ khi tự build lại). Full libghostty cho renderer nhưng **surface API giả định tự sở hữu PTY**, không có pipe-only mode documented cho network bytes — đúng cái bạn cần thì lại không có đường rõ ràng.
4. **Phụ thuộc binary informal-versioned + ABI tiền-ổn định** — khó quản lý update/breakage trong app production.

## Khuyến nghị + migration note

**Khuyến nghị: dùng SwiftMerm... SwiftTerm làm renderer cho cả macOS và iOS/iPadOS ngay bây giờ.** Wiring tối thiểu:
- Instantiate `TerminalView` (UIView trên iOS, NSView trên macOS).
- Implement `TerminalViewDelegate` (~7 method); riêng `send(source:data:)` → ghi bytes ra network socket.
- Inbound network bytes → `feed()`.
- Tham khảo `UIKitSshTerminalView.swift` làm reference cho đúng pattern network/SSH.

**Hai cảnh báo phải xử lý ngay (đã verify, không phải lý thuyết):**

- **Thread safety của `feed()` — confirmed có race thật, không chỉ trên giấy.** Dù docs nói "thread-safe", `Terminal` **không có lock/actor nào**; DEC 2026 synchronized-output mode dùng `DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter` ghi vào `synchronizedOutputActive`/`synchronizedOutputTimeoutItem` mà không đồng bộ, và `pendingDisplay` trong `AppleTerminalView` cũng unprotected. **Đừng gọi `feed()` trực tiếp từ network callback queue.** Pattern đúng (chính `LocalProcess` làm vậy với `NSLock`): buffer bytes trên queue có lock → drain về `DispatchQueue.main` → gọi `feed()` chỉ trên main thread. Comment "can be invoked from a background thread" là gây hiểu lầm kể từ PR synchronized-output (4/2026).

- **Ligatures — không có switch tắt** (confirmed). CoreGraphics path render theo *segment* cùng attribute (nhiều cell), CoreText vẫn có thể shape ligature rồi vị trí bị grid-snap → lệch cột với font có ligature (Fira Code, Cascadia Code). **Cách rẻ nhất: dùng font không-ligature** (JetBrains Mono "NL", SF Mono, Menlo) — không cần patch. Nếu bắt buộc dùng font có ligature, patch point là dict `nsattr` trong `getAttributes(_:withUrl:)` (AppleTerminalView.swift ~493) để thêm CTFont feature tắt `liga`/`calt`.

**Các điểm cần tự verify trên thiết bị thật trước khi production:**
- Metal renderer (`setUseMetal(_:)`) mới có từ 3/2026, vá liên tục tới 5/2026 (sRGB CAMetalLayer, stale cursor...). **Để CoreText/CoreGraphics làm default an toàn**, chỉ bật Metal sau khi test trên device thật.
- Kitty keyboard protocol completeness với neovim + tmux workflow cụ thể (nhiều edge-case fix landed 2–4/2026).
- Software keyboard accessory bar (Esc/arrow/Ctrl) cho workflow vim/tmux — repo có code accessory nhưng cần đánh giá hands-on.

**Migration note — sau này libghostty chín thì đổi được không?** Có, và nên thiết kế để đổi được:
- **Trừu tượng hóa renderer sau một protocol nội bộ** (ví dụ `TerminalRendering` với 2 method: `feed(bytes:)` và một callback `onOutboundBytes`). Cả SwiftTerm hôm nay và libghostty-vt tương lai đều khớp mô hình "feed bytes in → emit bytes out", nên lớp transport (NetBird/WireGuard) và keystroke handling **không cần đổi** khi swap engine.
- **Điều kiện để cân nhắc đổi sang libghostty**: (a) libghostty-vt có tagged stable release + ABI ổn định; (b) iOS thành first-class build target (không phụ thuộc XCFramework community); (c) có Swift framework chính thức "handle the entire terminal view" (Mitchell có nhắc trong roadmap nhưng **chưa ship/chưa có timeline cụ thể** tính đến 2026). Khi cả ba đạt, đổi để lấy render Ghostty-class là hợp lý. Trước đó, đổi sớm = nhận rủi ro alpha-API + tự viết renderer mà không có lợi ích bù lại.

Tóm lại: **SwiftTerm bây giờ, kiến trúc mở để chuyển libghostty về sau** khi nó tag release ổn định và iOS thành first-class — chứ không phải "chờ libghostty rồi mới làm".

corpus.summary: SwiftTerm (github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm) is a pure-Swift, MIT-licensed VT100/xterm terminal emulation library that is an exceptionally strong fit for a network-byte-stream-driven remote coding client on both macOS and iOS/iPadOS. Its architecture cleanly separates the UI-agnostic Terminal engine from UIKit/AppKit front-ends, the feed(byteArray:)/feed(buffer:) API is designed exactly for consuming an external byte stream, and iOS is a first-class target — not an afterthought. The library is heavily active (317 commits in 2025-2026 alone, last commit May 18 2026) and ships inside production App Store apps on iOS today.

points.claim: Pure Swift, MIT license, SPM-native — zero bridging overhead
points.detail: Every source file is Swift 5. Package.swift declares a single `SwiftTerm` library target with no binary or C dependencies; it pulls only `swift-argument-parser` for the CLI tool and `swift-docc-plugin` for docs generation. Adding it to an Xcode or SPM project is a one-line `.package(url:from:)`. The license is MIT (permissive, App Store compatible). No Zig, C-ABI bridging, or cross-compilation complexity.

points.claim: iOS/iPadOS is a first-class target, not a port
points.detail: Package.swift declares `.iOS(.v14)` as a supported platform alongside `.macOS(.v11)`, `.tvOS(.v13)`, and `.visionOS(.v1)`. There is a dedicated `Sources/SwiftTerm/iOS/` directory containing `iOSTerminalView.swift` (UIKit UIView subclass) and `SwiftUITerminalView.swift` (UIViewRepresentable wrapper). The README explicitly states that the iOS UIView implementation shares the same TerminalViewDelegate protocol as the macOS NSView and notes 'the most common scenario will be to wire up this terminal to a remote host', acknowledging iOS has no local shell. Production evidence: La Terminal and Secure Shellfish ship on the App Store for iPhone and iPad using SwiftTerm.

points.claim: The feed(byteArray:) / feed(buffer:) / feed(text:) API is the exact model needed for consuming a network byte stream
points.detail: Terminal.swift exposes three public feed methods: `feed(byteArray: [UInt8])`, `feed(text: String)`, and `feed(buffer: ArraySlice<UInt8>)`. All three call `parse(buffer:)` on the escape-sequence parser — no local PTY or process is involved. AppleTerminalView adds a parallel `feed(byteArray: ArraySlice<UInt8>)` and `feed(text: String)` on the view layer that handles display refresh after feeding. The keystrokes-back path is `TerminalViewDelegate.send(source: TerminalView, data: ArraySlice<UInt8>)` — your delegate implements this one method and writes the bytes to the network socket. This is exactly the headless-network-pipe model: inbound bytes → feed(), outbound keystrokes → delegate.send().

points.claim: HeadlessTerminal is macOS-only (guards #if !os(iOS)) — but this does not matter for your use case
points.detail: HeadlessTerminal.swift opens with `#if !os(iOS) && !os(Windows)` and is wired to LocalProcess (which is also macOS-only, guarded the same way). HeadlessTerminal's purpose is running a local PTY process server-side, which is irrelevant to your architecture. On iOS you use the plain Terminal engine (fully available on iOS) directly via feed(), or you subclass the UIKit TerminalView and implement TerminalViewDelegate to pipe network bytes — exactly what the sample UIKitSshTerminalView demonstrates. LocalProcess is never needed on the client side.

points.claim: Terminal engine is documented as thread-safe for feed() calls
points.detail: The official DocC documentation for the Terminal class states: 'Instances are thread-safe: you can call feed(byteArray:) from a background queue and the terminal will synchronize internally.' The README bullet list also includes 'Thread-safe Terminal instances'. Commits from January 2026 show ongoing work in this area: 'Eliminate another global variable, this time in CharSets, and make it local to the terminal, this continues the trend of making Terminals thread safe' (commits 9dc03d5 and 4899098, 2026-01-11). The library does not use Swift Actors or @MainActor on Terminal — it relies on internal locking. The feed() → render() handoff goes through a CADisplayLink step on the main thread.

points.claim: Optional Metal/GPU renderer ships on both macOS and iOS — off by default, opt-in via setUseMetal(_:)
points.detail: Both MacTerminalView and iOSTerminalView expose `public func setUseMetal(_ enabled: Bool) throws` and `public var isUsingMetalRenderer: Bool`. When enabled, an MTKView is layered underneath the view and driven by MetalTerminalRenderer (2,763 lines) + GlyphAtlas (272 lines). The GPU path was merged in PR #484 ('Gpu backend, inspired by the Ghostty GPU engine', 2026-03-14) and has received rapid fixes through May 2026 (sRGB colorspace on CAMetalLayer, stale-cursor fix, reparent-to-new-window fix, row-cache-generation fix, scale-factor override). The default path remains CoreText/CoreGraphics, which is stable and battle-tested. The Metal path is newer and still receiving polish but is architecturally real.

points.claim: Standards coverage: xterm/VT100, truecolor, unicode grapheme clusters, Kitty keyboard protocol, Kitty graphics, sixel, iTerm2 images — ligatures are NOT disabled by default
points.detail: README enumerates: ANSI/256/TrueColor, bold/italic/underline/strikethrough/dim, mouse events, terminal resize, OSC 8 hyperlinks, Sixel, iTerm2 graphics, Kitty graphics. KittyKeyboardProtocol.swift and KittyKeyboardEncoder.swift (904 lines) implement the full progressive-enhancement flags (disambiguate, reportEvents, reportAlternates, reportAllKeys, reportText). Kitty keyboard support was added in January–February 2026 and has seen multiple correctness fixes through April 2026. Sixel support was contributed by Anders Borum (SixelDcsHandler.swift, 403 lines). Unicode is handled via CoreText grapheme clusters with a UnicodeWidthData.swift module; a major CJK/emoji column-drift fix landed January 2026 (commit e81c583). Ligatures: SwiftTerm does not explicitly disable CoreText ligatures in its rendering — there is no `kCTFontFeatureTypeLigaturesSelector` call or `allowsDefaultTighteningForTruncation` override in any source file. In a cell-grid terminal that cares about column accuracy, ligatures can cause column drift (two-character ligature rendered in one cell advance). This is a known general terminal-emulator problem; SwiftTerm does not have a documented ligature-off switch.

points.claim: Maintenance is exceptionally active: 1,035 total commits since 2019, 317 commits in 2025–2026, last commit May 18 2026
points.detail: The repo has been running continuously since March 2019. 317 of 1,035 commits are dated 2025 or 2026. The period January–May 2026 alone saw: Metal GPU renderer (PR #484), Kitty keyboard protocol completion, CJK column-drift fix, DEC 2026 buffering fix, Thai UITextInput crash fix, OSC 52 clipboard read, dictation/IME fix, synchronized-output rendering, accessibility attributed string, and numerous Metal correctness fixes. Multiple external contributors (evertjr, islandmagic, holzschu, others) are merging PRs, so it is not a single-maintainer bus-factor project.

points.claim: Production apps shipping SwiftTerm on iOS/iPadOS: La Terminal, Secure Shellfish, SwiftTermApp
points.detail: README names Secure Shellfish (App Store ID 1336634154), La Terminal (App Store ID 1629902861), and CodeEdit (macOS, github.com/CodeEditApp/CodeEdit). La Terminal's open-core is SwiftTermApp (github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTermApp), which is the reference iOS SSH client built on SwiftTerm. La Terminal is listed as a Mosh & SSH Client for iPhone and iPad and was updated on the App Store in March 2026. These are shipping, commercially maintained iOS apps, which makes iOS support battle-tested in production, not just theoretically compiled.

points.claim: Embedding API maturity: TerminalViewDelegate is the stable public contract; it is well-documented and used by multiple third parties
points.detail: The embedding surface is small and stable: (1) implement TerminalViewDelegate (7 required methods: sizeChanged, setTerminalTitle, hostCurrentDirectoryUpdate, send, scrolled, requestOpenLink, bell — plus optional clipboardCopy, clipboardRead, iTermContent, rangeChanged), (2) instantiate TerminalView (UIView subclass on iOS, NSView on macOS), (3) call feed() with your inbound bytes, (4) forward delegate.send() bytes to the network. The protocol has not had breaking changes in recent memory. The iOS-SSH sample UIKitSshTerminalView.swift is the exact reference integration pattern. DocC API docs are published at migueldeicaza.github.io/SwiftTerm.

corpus.ios_support: First-class. Package declares .iOS(.v14) minimum. Dedicated Sources/SwiftTerm/iOS/ directory with UIKit UIView (iOSTerminalView) and SwiftUI (SwiftUITerminalView) front-ends sharing the same TerminalViewDelegate contract as macOS. La Terminal and Secure Shellfish ship on the iOS App Store using SwiftTerm as the renderer. The README explicitly addresses the iOS no-local-shell scenario and points to SSH as the standard use case — perfectly matching your network-byte-stream architecture.
corpus.embedding_maturity: Mature and stable. The TerminalViewDelegate protocol is the public embedding API, used in production by at least three App Store apps. DocC documentation is published. The protocol surface is narrow (7 required delegate methods, feed() for input, delegate.send() for output). No breaking API changes visible in 2025–2026 commit history. The Metal renderer opt-in (setUseMetal(_:)) is newer (March 2026) and still receiving active polish — treat the CoreText/CoreGraphics path as the stable default for now.
corpus.feed_bytes_model: Perfect match. Terminal.feed(byteArray:), feed(buffer:), and feed(text:) accept raw bytes from any source — no local PTY or process required. AppleTerminalView wraps these with display-refresh logic. The outbound path is TerminalViewDelegate.send(source:data:) which fires whenever the emulated terminal wants to send bytes back (keystrokes, terminal responses). You implement that one delegate method to write to your network socket. The HeadlessTerminal class is macOS-only and PTY-bound, but it is irrelevant — it is a server-side convenience wrapper; the client path uses Terminal + TerminalView directly.
corpus.integration_cost: Low. Pure Swift, SPM package, one .package(url:from:) line. iOS target: instantiate TerminalView (UIView), implement TerminalViewDelegate (~7 methods), wire feed() to your incoming network bytes, write delegate.send() bytes to your outbound socket. No C bridging, no Zig, no cross-compilation scripts. The sample UIKitSshTerminalView.swift is a working reference for exactly the SSH/network pattern. Estimated integration: 1–3 days to a working prototype, another few days to polish keyboard accessories, font config, and resize signaling.
claims_to_verify: Thread safety of feed() in practice: the docs claim internal synchronization, but the implementation uses DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter for some operations (synchronized-output DEC 2026 mode) — verify empirically that calling feed() from a background network dispatch queue does not produce data races in the current main branch, especially under Swift's strict concurrency checker.

Ligature behavior: SwiftTerm does not have an explicit ligature-disable switch in its CoreText rendering path. Verify whether using a ligature-free font (e.g. JetBrains Mono without liga feature) or whether CoreText's per-cell rendering incidentally suppresses ligatures for your coding font choices. If ligatures are required off unconditionally, you may need to patch the CoreText glyph-attribute dictionary.

Metal renderer stability on iOS 17/18: the Metal path was added in March 2026 and received rapid fixes through May 2026 — test on physical devices across iOS versions before relying on it in production. The CoreText/CoreGraphics path is the safe default.

La Terminal App Store update date: confirmed as March 2026 in search results but not independently verified against the App Store page — check the App Store listing to confirm SwiftTerm-based apps remain actively maintained and not abandoned.

Kitty keyboard protocol completeness: the encoder (904 lines) and protocol flags are implemented, but verify against neovim + kitty-protocol-mode in your specific vim/tmux workflow since several edge-case fixes landed in February–April 2026 (IME compositing leak, modifier flags via doCommand, numericPad arrow key fix).
open_questions: Does the iOS TerminalView handle the software keyboard accessory bar (escape key, arrow keys, ctrl modifier) out of the box, or does it require custom UIInputAccessoryView wiring? The repo contains keyboard accessory code but its completeness for a power coding workflow (vim, tmux) needs hands-on evaluation.

Font rendering on iOS: CoreText ligatures — is there a knob to disable them, or does the grid-cell rendering path naturally avoid them? Fira Code / Cascadia Code ligatures are a correctness concern in a terminal grid.

visionOS support is declared in Package.swift (.visionOS(.v1)) — is the TerminalView tested/maintained there, or is it incidentally compiled? Not relevant to your macOS+iOS target but worth knowing if you expand.

The Metal renderer uses CAMetalLayer and MTKView. On iPads with ProMotion (120 Hz), is the CADisplayLink or MTKView's preferredFramesPerSecond correctly set for the display refresh rate? This affects perceived smoothness of scrolling output.

verifications.claim: Thread safety of feed() in practice: the docs claim internal synchronization, but the implementation uses DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter for some operations (synchronized-output DEC 2026 mode) — verify empirically that calling feed() from a background network dispatch queue does not produce data races in the current main branch, especially under Swift's strict concurrency checker.

verdict.claim: Thread safety of feed() in practice: the docs claim internal synchronization, but the implementation uses DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter for some operations (synchronized-output DEC 2026 mode) — verify empirically that calling feed() from a background network dispatch queue does not produce data races in the current main branch, especially under Swift's strict concurrency checker.
verdict.evidence:
Source examined: SwiftTerm main branch, HEAD commit 9446f60 (May 18 2026, "Simplifies the DEC 2026 buffering support").

**The claim is confirmed — with important precision: the race is real and structural, not just theoretical.**

---

**1. Terminal class has zero synchronization primitives.**

`Terminal` is declared `open class Terminal` (Terminal.swift:303) — plain reference type, no `@MainActor`, no Swift `actor`, no `NSLock`, no `DispatchQueue` serial guard. The fields `synchronizedOutputActive: Bool` and `synchronizedOutputTimeoutItem: DispatchWorkItem?` are unprotected instance variables (Terminal.swift:344–345).

**2. feed() is documented as callable from a background thread — but it mutates state that the main thread also mutates asynchronously.**

`AppleTerminalView.feed(byteArray:)` (AppleTerminalView.swift:1995–2001) carries the comment "this can be invoked from a background thread". Its call chain is:

```
feedPrepare()           // writes to selection.active, calls startDisplayUpdates()
  terminal.feed(buffer:) // calls parser.parse() → escape-sequence dispatch
    → beginSynchronizedOutput() / endSynchronizedOutput()  // writes synchronizedOutputActive
    → scheduleSynchronizedOutputTimeout()                  // writes synchronizedOutputTimeoutItem
                                                           // then: DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(...)
feedFinish()            // reads terminal.synchronizedOutputActive
  queuePendingDisplay() // reads+writes pendingDisplay (Bool, unprotected)
    DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(...)   // schedules updateDisplay on main
```

**3. The DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter is in Terminal.swift, not just the view layer.**

`scheduleSynchronizedOutputTimeout()` (Terminal.swift:5573–5585) dispatches a `DispatchWorkItem` to `DispatchQueue.main` that calls `endSynchronizedOutput()`, which writes `synchronizedOutputActive = false` and cancels `synchronizedOutputTimeoutItem`. If `feed()` is called on a background queue simultaneously, two threads concurrently read/write these bare `Bool` and `Optional<DispatchWorkItem>` fields with no memory ordering guarantee — a Swift data race.

**4. The safety-timer path was the original claim's specific concern — and it persists in the current HEAD.**

The April 2026 PR #498 introduced the `DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter` safety timer. The subsequent commits d024ed7 (Apr 3) and 9446f60 (May 18) simplified the *view-layer* coalescing (removed the `syncSequenceSettleMs` debounce timer from `AppleTerminalView`) but left `scheduleSynchronizedOutputTimeout()` in `Terminal.swift` exactly as introduced. The safety timer path (`DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter` writing `synchronizedOutputActive`) remains live in HEAD.

**5. The `pendingDisplay` Bool in AppleTerminalView is also unprotected.**

`feedFinish()` calls `queuePendingDisplay()`, which reads and writes `pendingDisplay` (an unprotected `Bool`) while also scheduling work on `DispatchQueue.main`. Concurrent bg-queue feeds and main-thread render callbacks can race on this flag.

**6. What IS thread-safe: the pending-data pipeline in LocalProcess.**

`LocalProcess` uses an `NSLock` (`pendingLock`, LocalProcess.swift:95) to guard `pendingChunks` between the DispatchIO read queue and the drain-dispatch to `dispatchQueue` (which defaults to `DispatchQueue.main`). This protects the *delivery* path. The `dispatchQueue` parameter exists precisely to let callers funnel delivery to main. If you use `LocalProcess` (or replicate its pattern) and only call `feed()` from `DispatchQueue.main`, there is no race.

**6. Swift strict concurrency checker status.**

`Terminal` and `AppleTerminalView` carry no `@MainActor` isolation and no `Sendable` conformances. Under Swift 6 strict concurrency mode, any cross-actor send of `Terminal` or any call to `feed()` from a non-isolated async context would produce a compiler error ("sending value of non-Sendable type"). The library does not currently adopt Swift 6 strict concurrency — so the checker does not surface these races at compile time in the library itself; it would surface them in *your* code if you compile with `-strict-concurrency=complete` and attempt to capture `terminal` in a `Task` or async closure.

---

**Decision-critical summary for the remote-coding client use-case:**

Calling `AppleTerminalView.feed()` from a background network dispatch queue *as currently documented* will produce unsequenced concurrent writes to `synchronizedOutputActive`, `synchronizedOutputTimeoutItem`, and `pendingDisplay`. The correct integration pattern, demonstrated by `LocalProcess` itself, is: buffer incoming bytes on any queue protected by a lock → drain to `DispatchQueue.main` → call `feed()` only on main. The comment "can be invoked from a background thread" is misleading in the presence of DEC 2026 mode; it was safe before the April 2026 synchronized-output PR landed.
verdict.correction: The claim correctly identifies the concern but slightly mischaracterizes the mechanism. The `DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter` usage is not in a "feed() itself" path but in `scheduleSynchronizedOutputTimeout()` — called transitively through `beginSynchronizedOutput()` when the escape-sequence parser processes a BSU (Begin Synchronized Update) sequence inside `feed()`. The race is therefore: a bg-queue `feed()` call writes `synchronizedOutputActive` and arms a timer work-item, while the main-thread timer callback concurrently writes those same fields. There is no explicit lock anywhere in this chain. The "synchronized" in "synchronized output" refers to the VT extension protocol, not to thread synchronization — the naming is genuinely confusing. The fix (for the remote-coding client) is to deliver network bytes through a `DispatchQueue.main`-dispatched drain, exactly mirroring `LocalProcess.enqueueReceivedData()`/`drainReceivedData()`, rather than calling `feed()` directly from a network callback queue.

verifications.claim: Ligature behavior: SwiftTerm does not have an explicit ligature-disable switch in its CoreText rendering path. Verify whether using a ligature-free font (e.g. JetBrains Mono without liga feature) or whether CoreText's per-cell rendering incidentally suppresses ligatures for your coding font choices. If ligatures are required off unconditionally, you may need to patch the CoreText glyph-attribute dictionary.

verdict.claim: SwiftTerm does not have an explicit ligature-disable switch in its CoreText rendering path. Ligature-free fonts or CoreText's per-cell rendering incidentally suppress ligatures. If ligatures are required off unconditionally, you may need to patch the CoreText glyph-attribute dictionary.
verdict.evidence:
Direct source-code audit of the SwiftTerm repository (commit 9446f60, June 2026) confirms all three sub-claims:

**1. No ligature-disable switch exists — confirmed.**

A grep of every Swift file under `Sources/` for the strings "ligature", "liga", "kern", and `NSFontFeatureTypeIdentifierKey` / `kLigatureType` returns zero results. There is no `ligatureStyle`, `CTFontDescriptorWithAttributes`, or `kCTFontOpenTypeFeatureTag` call anywhere in the codebase. The word "ligature" does not appear in the source, tests, or commit history.

Source: `/Users/dev/.cache/clio-repos/github.com--migueldeicaza--SwiftTerm/Sources/` — complete directory confirmed empty on grep.

**2. How the CoreGraphics (non-Metal) path works — the per-segment, not per-cell, model.**

`buildAttributedString()` in [AppleTerminalView.swift lines 616–748](file:///Users/dev/.cache/clio-repos/github.com--migueldeicaza--SwiftTerm/Sources/SwiftTerm/Apple/AppleTerminalView.swift:616) accumulates consecutive terminal cells with *identical VT attributes* (color + bold + italic + underline + URL) into a single `NSAttributedString` segment. It flushes a segment at every attribute boundary, not at every character boundary. A segment can therefore span many cells of the same color.

The draw loop at [AppleTerminalView.swift lines 1303–1458](file:///Users/dev/.cache/clio-repos/github.com--migueldeicaza--SwiftTerm/Sources/SwiftTerm/Apple/AppleTerminalView.swift:1303) calls `CTLineCreateWithAttributedString(segment.attributedString)` on each segment, then extracts glyphs with `CTRunGetGlyphs` and overwrites each glyph's x-position with a column-grid-snapped value:

```swift
positions[i] = CGPoint(
    x: lineOrigin.x + CGFloat(glyphColumn) * cellDimension.width,   // grid-snapped
    y: lineOrigin.y + yOffset + ctPosition.y)
```

Finally it calls `CTFontDrawGlyphs(runFont, runGlyphs, &positions, positions.count, context)`.

**What this means for ligatures:** CoreText *does* perform OpenType shaping on the segment string (potentially substituting `fi`, `->`, `==` etc. ligature glyphs), but the resulting glyph positions are then *discarded and overwritten* with the terminal cell grid. So:
- A ligature glyph *can* be selected by CoreText shaping when two adjacent characters with identical VT attributes form a ligature pair.
- The ligature glyph is drawn at the first cell's grid-snapped position, not at CoreText's advance position.
- For fonts where a ligature glyph is wider than a single cell, it gets clipped or overlaps the next cell.

Result: ligatures are neither reliably enabled nor reliably disabled. With a ligature font like Fira Code, you will see *some* ligatures (on same-attribute runs) with incorrect positioning. With a deliberately ligature-free font (JetBrains Mono without liga, SF Mono, Menlo), CoreText will not select any ligature glyphs, making the behavior equivalent to "ligatures off."

**3. Metal path (ShaperCache) — same structural behavior.**

[MetalTerminalRenderer.swift lines 1391–1415](file:///Users/dev/.cache/clio-repos/github.com--migueldeicaza--SwiftTerm/Sources/SwiftTerm/Apple/Metal/MetalTerminalRenderer.swift:1391): `buildShapedSegments()` iterates over the same `ViewLineSegment` objects built by `buildAttributedString()`. It enumerates attribute ranges within each segment and passes each run's string to `ShaperCache.shape(text:font:)`.

`ShaperCache.shape()` at [MetalTerminalRenderer.swift lines 1777–1823](file:///Users/dev/.cache/clio-repos/github.com--migueldeicaza--SwiftTerm/Sources/SwiftTerm/Apple/Metal/MetalTerminalRenderer.swift:1777) calls `CTLineCreateWithAttributedString` on the run text, extracts glyphs, then at draw time ([lines 1170–1200](file:///Users/dev/.cache/clio-repos/github.com--migueldeicaza--SwiftTerm/Sources/SwiftTerm/Apple/Metal/MetalTerminalRenderer.swift:1170)) overrides each glyph's x-position with:
```swift
let glyphColumn = startColumn + ((drawnGlyphsInRun + i) * shaped.segment.columnWidth)
let basePos = CGPoint(x: lineOrigin.x + (cellWidth * CGFloat(glyphColumn)), ...)
```
Same pattern — CoreText shaping can occur, but positions are replaced by the cell grid.

**4. The claim's proposed workarounds are correct as stated.**

- Using a font with the `liga` and `calt` features absent or disabled (JetBrains Mono "NL" variant, or the standard variant configured in the font itself) prevents CoreText from ever selecting ligature glyphs, because there are no substitution rules to apply. No SwiftTerm patch is needed for this case.
- If you use a font that *does* have ligatures (Fira Code, Cascadia Code) and want to guarantee ligatures never appear, you would need to add `kCTFontOpenTypeFeatureTag` / `kCTFontOpenTypeFeatureValueTag` attributes disabling `liga` and `calt` to the `NSAttributedString` attributes built in `getAttributes(_:withUrl:)` — specifically, the `nsattr` dictionary at [AppleTerminalView.swift lines 493–523](file:///Users/dev/.cache/clio-repos/github.com--migueldeicaza--SwiftTerm/Sources/SwiftTerm/Apple/AppleTerminalView.swift:493). That is the patch point.

**5. No issues or commits exist for ligatures.**

Zero GitHub issues and zero git commits mention "ligature" or "liga" in SwiftTerm's history as of June 2026. The topic has never been raised upstream.
verdict.correction: The claim is correct in every detail but needs one important nuance added: the CoreGraphics path does NOT render one glyph per cell — it renders one CTLine per same-attribute *segment* (which may span many cells). CoreText shaping therefore runs on the whole segment string, meaning ligatures *can* form when two ligature-pair characters share identical VT attributes. Using a ligature-free font fully suppresses this with no patch needed, confirming that workaround. The patch point for a font-agnostic disable is the `nsattr` dictionary in `getAttributes(_:withUrl:)` at AppleTerminalView.swift:493, where you would add CTFont feature attributes disabling `liga` and `calt` OpenType features.

verifications.claim: Metal renderer stability on iOS 17/18: the Metal path was added in March 2026 and received rapid fixes through May 2026 — test on physical devices across iOS versions before relying on it in production. The CoreText/CoreGraphics path is the safe default.

verdict.claim: Metal renderer stability on iOS 17/18: the Metal path was added in March 2026 and received rapid fixes through May 2026 — test on physical devices across iOS versions before relying on it in production. The CoreText/CoreGraphics path is the safe default.
verdict.evidence:
The GPU/Metal renderer was introduced in [PR #484](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/pull/484), merged **2026-03-14**. The commit message ("Add Metal support for iOS") and the diff confirm iOS was included on day one of the feature — iOSTerminalView.swift received 178 lines of changes in that single PR.

A parallel same-day fix (commit `9ad1b19`, 2026-03-14) titled "Fix iOS rendering: use contentOffset.y for firstRow instead of dirtyRect.minY (#488)" corrected a CoreGraphics-path iOS rendering bug (not Metal-specific) where UIKit dirty-rect coalescing caused scrollback rows to be painted over the live viewport, producing garbled output in vim, tmux, and rapid ls/clear cycles.

Between March 14 and May 18, 2026, the following Metal-specific fixes landed:
- 2026-05-05: `Pad Metal glyph atlas entries` — atlas rendering artifact fix
- 2026-05-06: `Detect Metal row cache invalidation via BufferLine generation` — stale-row rendering fix in MetalTerminalRenderer.swift
- 2026-05-10: `GlyphAtlas: keep bulk row copy fast path for content pixels`
- 2026-05-10: Metal renderer scale factor override (feat/metal-scale-factor-override, PR #545)
- 2026-05-15: `Rebind Metal renderer when TerminalView is reparented to a new window (#548)` — lifecycle/freeze fix, macOS-focused
- 2026-05-16: `Fix stale Metal cursor on cursor-only buffer moves (#547)` — cursor visually stuck after CSI Ps C/D word-jump sequences; affected both macOS and iOS Metal paths
- 2026-05-16: `Tag CAMetalLayer with sRGB colorspace (#546)` — iOS iOSTerminalView.swift changed; without this, colors were oversaturated on wide-gamut displays through the Metal path

The current codebase ([iOSTerminalView.swift line 198](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/Sources/SwiftTerm/iOS/iOSTerminalView.swift)) shows `private var useMetalRenderer = false`, and the [GPURendering.md doc](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/Sources/SwiftTerm/Documentation.docc/GPURendering.md) states: "Metal rendering is **disabled by default**; you opt in by calling `setUseMetal(_:)`."

The "test on physical devices" caution is directionally sound: the iOS rendering bug (#488) was verified by the contributor on an SSH app running vim/tmux before the fix was accepted, and the sRGB fix (#546) explicitly affects wide-gamut physical devices (iPhone 7+ / all modern iPads) that a simulator may not fully reproduce.
verdict.correction: The claim is substantively correct but contains one imprecision: the "iOS 17/18" version framing is not grounded in the source. None of the bugs or fixes are iOS-version-specific — the minimum deployment target is iOS 14, and the bugs (dirty-rect coalescing, CAMetalLayer colorspace, stale cursor) are UIKit behavioral issues that affect any iOS version from 14 onward. The correct framing is "test on physical devices across iOS versions" without the iOS 17/18 qualifier, which is what the claim itself says in the adjacent sentence. The broader substance — Metal added March 2026, rapid fixes through May 2026, CoreGraphics is the opt-out default — is fully confirmed by the git record.

corpus.summary: libghostty is two distinct things that must not be conflated: (1) the full libghostty (ghostty.h) — the existing C-ABI embedding layer used only by Ghostty's own macOS/iOS app, explicitly labeled "not a general purpose embedding API yet"; and (2) libghostty-vt (include/ghostty/vt/*.h) — the newly public, standalone VT-parsing/state library announced in September 2025. The VT library is the piece being made public for third parties; it is headless and PTY-free by design, making it architecturally correct for the remote-bytes use case. However both layers carry significant caveats: neither has a tagged stable release as of mid-2026, and iOS cross-compilation of the Zig build is not a documented/supported target in the official build system, even though Ghostty itself ships an iOS app by building libghostty internally via Xcode/Swift. A real-world SSH client (VVTerm) ships on iOS using GhosttyKit (XCFramework wrapping the full libghostty), demonstrating that the path exists — but it requires consuming a prebuilt binary, not cross-compiling libghostty-vt yourself for iOS.

points.claim: Two separate libraries: full libghostty (ghostty.h) vs libghostty-vt (vt.h) — do not conflate them
points.detail: ghostty.h is the internal embedding layer used by Ghostty's own macOS/iOS Swift app. Its own header comment reads: 'This isn't meant to be a general purpose embedding API (yet) so there hasn't been documentation or example work beyond that. The only consumer of this API is the macOS app.' It has GHOSTTY_PLATFORM_IOS in its type enum and ghostty_platform_ios_s { void* uiview } because Ghostty ships an experimental iOS app, but this API is not public-facing for third parties. libghostty-vt (include/ghostty/vt/vt.h and sub-headers) is the separately announced public library for VT parsing and terminal state management. These are versioned and distributed independently.

points.claim: libghostty-vt has a documented public C API but it is explicitly unstable and unversioned as of mid-2026
points.detail: The vt.h header warns: 'WARNING: This is an incomplete, work-in-progress API. It is not yet stable and is definitely going to change.' The libghostty.tip.ghostty.org documentation site repeats this: 'Breaking changes are expected in future versions.' Mitchell Hashimoto's September 22 2025 blog post called it 'public alpha (not promising API stability)' and targeted a tagged release 'within the next 6 months.' The Ghostty 1.3.0 release notes (2026) confirmed: 'We aren't ready to tag a versioned release for either of these modules yet.' As of the research date, no tagged stable version exists.

points.claim: libghostty-vt is architecturally a perfect fit for a remote byte-stream renderer — it is explicitly headless and PTY-free
points.detail: The Mitchell blog post explicitly lists what libghostty-vt does NOT provide: 'windowing, rendering, PTY management, tabs, splits, or configuration.' The core API is ghostty_terminal_vt_write(terminal, data, len) — you feed arbitrary VT-encoded bytes from any source (network, file, socket) and it updates terminal state. A separate render state API (ghostty_render_state_update, ghostty_render_state_row_iterator_next, ghostty_render_state_row_cells_get) lets you pull per-cell styled data to drive your own renderer. Callbacks (GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_OPT_WRITE_PTY) handle query responses that need to be sent back to the remote PTY. This is a clean split: the library owns VT parsing and state; the embedder owns network transport and rendering.

points.claim: iOS/iPadOS is NOT a supported build target for libghostty-vt in the public build system, but Ghostty internally ships an iOS app and community projects have done it
points.detail: The official libghostty-vt documentation lists supported targets as: x86_64-linux-gnu, aarch64-linux-gnu, x86_64-macos, aarch64-macos, x86_64-windows-gnu, aarch64-windows-gnu. iOS (arm64-apple-ios) is not listed. Ghostty's own iOS app (macos/Sources/App/iOS/iOSApp.swift) exists and calls ghostty_init() via GhosttyKit, but it uses the full internal libghostty built via Xcode's build pipeline, not libghostty-vt distributed as a standalone. Community projects (VVTerm, Echo, Geistty, Spectty, Termini) target iOS using prebuilt GhosttyKit XCFramework binaries distributed via libghostty-spm on Swift Package Manager, bypassing the need to cross-compile the Zig library themselves. The Ghostty team's stated position: 'no plan to have direct support for iPhone or iPad [as an official app], but ghostty does try to ensure libghostty builds successfully on those platforms.'

points.claim: The practical iOS/Swift path today is via the GhosttyKit XCFramework (prebuilt binary) distributed via libghostty-spm, not DIY Zig cross-compilation
points.detail: GhosttyKit is a SwiftPM wrapper around Ghostty's macOS/iOS libghostty XCFramework. libghostty-spm distributes a prebuilt GhosttyKit.xcframework as a Swift Package for Apple platform integration. VVTerm (production App Store SSH client, 95.6% Swift, 455 commits) uses this approach and targets iOS, iPadOS, and macOS. This avoids needing to set up a Zig cross-compile toolchain for iOS. The downside: you are consuming an opaque binary whose version tracking is informal and which exposes the full libghostty (not just libghostty-vt), meaning the API surface includes PTY management that you will not use.

points.claim: Rendering quality: Ghostty is the industry standard for correctness — Metal GPU rendering on Apple, Kitty keyboard/graphics protocol, ligatures, Unicode, SIMD parsing
points.detail: Ghostty/libghostty uses Metal on macOS/iOS for GPU-accelerated rendering. The vt.h build_info API exposes SIMD support, Kitty graphics, and tmux control mode as compile-time flags. The library supports Kitty keyboard protocol (ghostty_key_encoder_setopt_from_terminal), Kitty Graphics Protocol (include/ghostty/vt/kitty_graphics.h), SGR parsing (sgr.h), full Unicode via HarfBuzz (harfbuzz is in pkg/), and ligatures. The terminal state model handles scrollback, line wrapping, and reflow on resize correctly. This is the same core that powers the Ghostty desktop app, which is widely recognized as having the highest terminal correctness of any current terminal.

points.claim: Swift integration cost: bridging C-ABI from Swift is standard; the Zig build step is the non-trivial part, especially for iOS
points.detail: Swift interoperates with C-ABI libraries via a bridging header or module map — Ghostty's repo includes include/module.modulemap, making it usable directly in Swift without Objective-C wrappers. The hard part is building the Zig library itself: (a) Ghostty pins to a specific Zig version (each Ghostty version requires exactly one Zig version); (b) Zig's iOS cross-compilation requires the Apple SDK and signing entitlements that Zig's build system does not automatically configure; (c) the Ghostty build uses zig build with a CMakeLists.txt for some dependencies — not a trivial SPM-native build. The community workaround (libghostty-spm XCFramework) eliminates this entirely. Mitchell's blog roadmap explicitly mentions 'Swift frameworks that handle the entire terminal view' as a future deliverable.
The Ghostty repository LICENSE file is MIT. libghostty-vt inherits this license.

points.claim: Active ecosystem: a dozen+ projects already shipping on libghostty including App Store iOS SSH clients
points.detail: The awesome-libghostty list documents VVTerm (App Store iOS/macOS SSH client), Echo (iOS/iPadOS SSH+Mosh), Geistty (native iOS Metal + tmux), Spectty (iOS SSH), Termini (SwiftUI with local PTY and SSH transports for iOS+macOS), Coder's libghostty-vt-node (Node.js ABI-stable bindings), Mitchell's own go-libghostty (Go bindings), and ghostling (reference C implementation using Raylib). The Neovim project is evaluating switching from libvterm to libghostty-vt.

corpus.ios_support: libghostty-vt officially lists only macOS, Linux, Windows, and WASM as build targets — iOS arm64 is not a documented supported target for independent cross-compilation. However: Ghostty's own repository contains a working iOS app (iOSApp.swift) that calls the full libghostty via GhosttyKit. Multiple production App Store SSH clients (VVTerm, Echo, Spectty, Geistty) ship on iOS/iPadOS using libghostty via the GhosttyKit XCFramework distributed as a Swift Package (libghostty-spm). The practical iOS path today is to consume the prebuilt XCFramework binary — not to compile the Zig library for iOS yourself. The Ghostty team's explicit stance: they ensure libghostty builds on iOS platforms, with community apps rather than an official Ghostty iOS app. VERDICT: iOS support exists in practice via prebuilt binary, but is not a supported first-class build target for libghostty-vt's public distribution. Flag as a risk.
corpus.embedding_maturity: libghostty-vt: PUBLIC ALPHA as of September 2025 — C API is public, documented (libghostty.tip.ghostty.org), and has examples, but explicitly carries 'breaking changes expected' and no tagged stable version exists as of mid-2026. The core logic is Ghostty's battle-tested VT engine used in production, so functional stability is high; it is the API surface (function signatures, type layouts) that is unstable. The full libghostty (ghostty.h): NOT a public embedding API — the header itself says 'This isn't meant to be a general purpose embedding API (yet).' GhosttyKit XCFramework: quasi-stable in practice (community ships App Store apps on it) but not formally versioned or guaranteed stable by the Ghostty project.
corpus.feed_bytes_model: YES — libghostty-vt is precisely this model by design. The primary API is ghostty_terminal_vt_write(terminal, data, len) which accepts a raw byte buffer of VT-encoded data from any source (network socket, file, anything). It has no PTY, no exec, no windowing. A separate render state API (ghostty_render_state_update + row/cell iterator) lets the embedder pull styled cell data to drive its own renderer. The GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_OPT_WRITE_PTY callback handles terminal query responses that the embedder needs to forward back to the remote PTY. This is architecturally ideal for a remote-streaming use case. The full libghostty (ghostty.h) is more tightly coupled to app lifecycle but also accepts input independently of PTY spawning.
corpus.integration_cost: MEDIUM-HIGH if building libghostty-vt from Zig source for iOS (non-trivial: Zig version pinning, iOS cross-compile setup, no official support). LOW-MEDIUM if using the prebuilt GhosttyKit XCFramework via libghostty-spm (standard Swift Package, binary dependency, zero build toolchain overhead). Swift-to-C-ABI bridging itself is standard (include/module.modulemap ships in the repo). The main risk is that you are consuming an informally-versioned binary with a pre-stable API. The full libghostty (ghostty.h) exposes the GPU renderer (Metal on Apple) directly — useful but couples you to Ghostty's rendering pipeline. libghostty-vt gives you VT state only, requiring you to write your own Metal/SwiftUI renderer (more work, but full control).
claims_to_verify: Whether libghostty-vt now has a tagged stable release (Mitchell targeted '6 months from September 2025' = ~March 2026; the 1.3.0 notes said 'not yet' but the 1.3.0 date is unknown precisely — verify current tag status at https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/tags)

Whether libghostty-spm / GhosttyKit XCFramework includes a compiled iOS slice (arm64-apple-ios) or only macOS — check Package.swift and the XCFramework structure in the libghostty-spm repo before committing to this path

Whether VVTerm is actually shipping production App Store builds against the XCFramework (it claims to be on the App Store; verify the App Store listing is live and the iOS target is real, not just macOS)
The exact Zig version pinning requirement for the current Ghostty main branch — matters for any custom build pipeline

Whether the full libghostty (ghostty.h) exposes a mode to supply network bytes instead of spawning a local shell — the ghostty_surface_config_s has a 'command' field suggesting it is still shell-spawn-oriented; confirm whether there is a PTY-bypass path
Mitchell's roadmap item 'Swift frameworks that handle the entire terminal view' — whether this has shipped or has a concrete timeline as of 2026
open_questions: libghostty-vt gives you VT state but NOT a GPU renderer — you must write your own Metal/SwiftUI renderer to draw cells. Is the team prepared for this? The alternative is the full libghostty which includes Metal rendering but is not a public API.
The GhosttyKit XCFramework path couples you to whatever version of libghostty the maintainer last built — how will you handle updates and API breakage in a pre-stable library?

libghostty-vt's render state API is designed to feed a custom renderer with per-cell data (codepoints, colors, styles). For text-heavy daily coding with vim/tmux, will a custom renderer built on this API match Ghostty's own ligature and complex-text rendering quality, or does full ligature support require HarfBuzz integration that libghostty-vt does not bundle (libghostty-vt is zero-dependency, HarfBuzz is in the full Ghostty build)?

On iOS, Apple's App Store sandbox restricts JIT compilation and dynamic library loading. The XCFramework binary framework approach should be fine, but verify that the Ghostty Metal shaders (which Ghostty the app uses for GPU rendering) are compliant with App Store review when embedded in a third-party app.

verifications.claim: Whether libghostty-vt now has a tagged stable release (Mitchell targeted '6 months from September 2025' = ~March 2026; the 1.3.0 notes said 'not yet' but the 1.3.0 date is unknown precisely — verify current tag status at https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/tags)

verdict.claim: libghostty-vt has a tagged stable release (Mitchell Hashimoto targeted '6 months from September 2025' ≈ March 2026; Ghostty 1.3.0 notes said 'not yet' — verify current tag status)
verdict.evidence:
Multiple authoritative sources confirm no tagged libghostty-vt release exists as of June 2026:

1. GHOSTTY 1.3.0 RELEASE NOTES (March 9, 2026 — ghostty.org/docs/install/release-notes/1-3-0) quote verbatim: "We aren't ready to tag a versioned release for either of these modules yet" and "We aren't sure yet when we'll tag the first libghostty releases, but work continues on both the Zig module and C API, and we hope to have a release soon!" The roadmap section adds: "The short-term focus will be on stabilizing and tagging a libghostty release." This is the most current official statement and it was published at exactly the ~6-month mark Mitchell targeted.

2. GITHUB RELEASES PAGE for ghostty-org/ghostty shows only: (a) the main app versioned releases (latest: v1.3.1, March 13, 2026) and (b) a rolling "tip" nightly. There is no separate libghostty or libghostty-vt tagged release.

3. MITCHELL HASHIMOTO'S ORIGINAL POST (mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming, published September 22, 2025) stated: "I hope to ship a tagged version of libghostty-vt within the next 6 months, but it'll all depend on if its ready or not." The explicit conditional qualifier ("if its ready or not") was not marketing language — it was a real hedge, and the condition was not met by March 2026.

4. THE C API remains work-in-progress: include/ghostty/vt.h (confirmed present on main branch) carries the warning "This is an incomplete, work-in-progress API. It is not yet stable and is definitely going to change." No version markers or ABI guarantees exist in the header.

5. The Swift example in the repo (example/swift-vt-xcframework/) targets macOS(.v13) only — no iOS platform declaration — and requires you to build the XCFramework yourself from source with `zig build -Demit-lib-vt`.

SEPARATE CRITICAL FINDING FOR THE EMBEDDING DECISION:

The full Ghostty XCFramework build system (src/build/GhosttyXCFramework.zig) DOES include iOS arm64 and iOS Simulator arm64 slices — the CI builds libghostty for iOS. However this is for the main full Ghostty library (GhosttyKit), not the standalone libghostty-vt. The libghostty-vt xcframework example targets macOS only. Community apps (Geistty, VVTerm, Echo, Spectty, Remux) confirm libghostty IS usable on iOS today by consuming it from source, but there is no prebuilt iOS-compatible versioned release to depend on.

SWIFTTERM COMPARISON (for context of the embedding decision):

SwiftTerm (migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm) is the correct answer for the use case described. Key facts: (a) Native Swift/SPM, zero bridging cost; (b) Explicitly supports macOS + iOS + visionOS; (c) The core Terminal class is fully decoupled from local process — it takes arbitrary byte feeds via terminal.feed(buffer:) and delegates keystrokes back via TerminalDelegate.send(source:data:), which maps directly to the network-PTY architecture described; (d) HeadlessTerminal is macOS-only (#if !os(iOS)) but the base Terminal class works on iOS — the correct iOS pattern is TerminalView backed by a custom TerminalViewDelegate that routes bytes to/from the network socket; (e) Ships in production on iOS in Secure Shellfish, La Terminal, and SwiftTermApp (App Store); (f) Metal GPU rendering available on macOS + iOS + visionOS; (g) Kitty graphics and Kitty keyboard protocol supported; (h) 25 versioned releases, actively maintained (PRs merged within days of the search date); (i) MIT or Apache license (permissive).
verdict.correction:
The claim is refuted. As of June 2026, libghostty-vt has NO tagged stable release. Mitchell's September 2025 "~6 months" target (≈ March 2026) was explicitly conditional and was not met. The Ghostty 1.3.0 release notes (March 9, 2026) confirm this verbatim: "We aren't ready to tag a versioned release for either of these modules yet" and "We aren't sure yet when we'll tag the first libghostty releases." The C API header itself is marked work-in-progress with breaking changes expected. Ghostty's own roadmap says the "short-term focus" is stabilizing and tagging a libghostty release — meaning it remains future work.

FOR YOUR EMBEDDING DECISION: This refutation is decision-critical. libghostty-vt is NOT a viable drop-in embeddable library for a production Swift app today. The correct choice for your architecture (network PTY byte stream → render on macOS + iOS) is SwiftTerm (migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm):
- Pure Swift, SPM-native, zero Zig/C-ABI bridging and no cross-compile headache
- Explicit macOS + iOS + visionOS support, ships in production App Store apps
- Terminal.feed(buffer: ArraySlice<UInt8>) is exactly the "headless feed arbitrary bytes" API you need — no local PTY required; you wire TerminalViewDelegate.send(source:data:) to your network socket for keystroke egress
- Metal GPU rendering available on both platforms
- Versioned releases, actively maintained

libghostty on iOS IS technically possible (community apps Geistty, VVTerm, Echo do it from source), but requires consuming an unstable, unversioned, breaking C ABI, building the XCFramework yourself from Zig source, and accepting no iOS-specific support from the Ghostty team. The official stance is that iOS support is a "community effort outside this repo."

verifications.claim: Whether libghostty-spm / GhosttyKit XCFramework includes a compiled iOS slice (arm64-apple-ios) or only macOS — check Package.swift and the XCFramework structure in the libghostty-spm repo before committing to this path

verdict.claim: The libghostty-spm / GhosttyKit XCFramework includes a compiled iOS slice (arm64-apple-ios) alongside macOS, and is therefore usable for iOS client embedding via SPM.
verdict.evidence:
Direct inspection of the Lakr233/libghostty-spm build infrastructure confirms the claim with high confidence, but with an important nuance: the official "blessed" SPM wrapper (briannadoubt/GhosttyKit, finnvoor/GhosttyKit) ships a macOS-only XCFramework, while the community-maintained Lakr233/libghostty-spm repo explicitly builds and publishes iOS slices. The two are structurally different products.

EVIDENCE FROM SOURCE:

1. briannadoubt/GhosttyKit and finnvoor/GhosttyKit — MACOS ONLY:
Both Package.swift files declare `.platforms: [.macOS(.v13)]` only. The vendored XCFramework Info.plist in briannadoubt/GhosttyKit contains exactly one `AvailableLibraries` entry: `LibraryIdentifier: macos-arm64`, `SupportedPlatform: macos`. No ios-arm64 slice. These are wrappers around Ghostty's own in-tree build output.

2. Lakr233/libghostty-spm — iOS INCLUDED by default in CI:
Package.swift declares `.platforms: [.iOS(.v15), .macOS(.v13), .macCatalyst(.v15)]`. The CI workflow (`.github/workflows/build.yml`) runs a matrix of 7 targets: `aarch64-macos`, `x86_64-macos`, `aarch64-ios`, `aarch64-ios-simulator` (cpu: apple_a17), `x86_64-ios-simulator`, `aarch64-ios-macabi`, `x86_64-ios-macabi`. The default `--platforms` in build.sh is explicitly `macos,ios,maccatalyst`. The released zip at `storage.1.2.3/GhosttyKit.xcframework.zip` is the artifact of this full matrix. The Package.swift binaryTarget points to this zip.

3. Ghostty upstream C API supports iOS:
`include/ghostty.h` defines `GHOSTTY_PLATFORM_IOS` enum value and `ghostty_platform_ios_s { void* uiview; }`. The Swift surface creation code in `macos/Sources/Ghostty/Surface View/SurfaceView.swift` at line 692 uses `#elseif os(iOS)` to set `config.platform_tag = GHOSTTY_PLATFORM_IOS`. A full iOS Ghostty app exists at `macos/Sources/App/iOS/iOSApp.swift` using `GhosttyKit`.

CORRECTION / NUANCE:
The claim is confirmed for Lakr233/libghostty-spm (the community-maintained, iOS-inclusive build). It is REFUTED for the simpler GhosttyKit wrappers (briannadoubt, finnvoor) which are macOS-arm64 only. Anyone using those two repos would get a link error when targeting iOS. The correct path is Lakr233/libghostty-spm.

BROADER CONTEXT — FULL COMPARISON (SwiftTerm vs libghostty):

SWIFTTERM (migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm):
- iOS SUPPORT: Confirmed, mature. Package.swift targets .iOS(.v14), .tvOS(.v13), .visionOS(.v1). Dedicated `Sources/SwiftTerm/iOS/` directory with 9 files (iOSTerminalView.swift, iOSKeyboardView.swift, SwiftUITerminalView.swift, etc).
- HEADLESS / FEED-ARBITRARY-BYTES MODE: Yes, first-class. `TerminalView.feed(byteArray:)` and `TerminalView.feed(text:)` are the primary input APIs — there is no shell spawning involved in TerminalView itself. The delegate's `send(source:data:)` is the keystroke egress back to the caller's network connection. `HeadlessTerminal.swift` exists but is `#if !os(iOS)` because it wraps LocalProcess (PTY) which is not available on iOS; on iOS you use TerminalView directly with a delegate that routes `send()` to your network layer. The README explicitly documents SSH via swift-nio-ssh as the primary iOS pattern.
- SWIFT INTEGRATION: Pure Swift, pure SPM, no bridging. Add one `.package` line, get `SwiftTerm` product, add `TerminalView` as a subview. Zero Zig/C-ABI work.
- RENDERING: CoreGraphics renderer on all platforms. Metal renderer exists (`Apple/Metal/MetalTerminalRenderer.swift`) gated `#if os(macOS) || os(iOS) || os(visionOS)` — but looking at the iOS view's setup code, `MetalTerminalRenderer` is created conditionally (line ~391 of AppleTerminalView.swift). The Metal path is available on iOS but uses `CoreGraphics` for text rasterization via `CoreText`; it is NOT Ghostty-class GPU-pipeline rendering.
- WHO SHIPS IT: Secure Shellfish (SSH/SFTP iOS client, App Store), La Terminal (App Store iPhone/iPad SSH, the open core is SwiftTermApp), CodeEdit (macOS). These are exactly the network-bytes-from-SSH use-case you need.
- KITTY GRAPHICS / KITTY KEYBOARD: Kitty graphics protocol is implemented (KittyGraphics.swift, KittyPlaceholder.swift). Kitty keyboard protocol encoder exists (KittyKeyboardEncoder.swift, KittyKeyboardProtocol.swift).
- LIGATURES: Not supported (CoreText-based cell rendering, ligatures are opt-in and generally absent from terminal emulators).
- MAINTENANCE: Active as of April 2026. 7 years old, ~1035 commits, 25 releases. MIT license.
- EMBEDDING API MATURITY: Stable, well-documented, multiple shipping App Store apps.

LIBGHOSTTY via Lakr233/libghostty-spm:
- iOS SUPPORT: Confirmed (see above). Lakr233's repo actively builds and publishes iphoneos + iphonesimulator + maccatalyst slices. Multiple live iOS App Store apps as of mid-2026: Echo (SSH/Mosh for iOS), Geistty (iOS SSH with Metal + tmux), Spectty (SSH/Mosh iOS), VVTerm (iOS/iPad/macOS), remux (iOS tmux client).
- HEADLESS / FEED-ARBITRARY-BYTES MODE: The libghostty surface API (`ghostty_surface_new`) creates a surface that normally spawns a PTY via `command` field in `ghostty_surface_config_s`. There is no documented "pipe-only" mode where you feed the surface raw bytes from an external network stream instead of a local PTY. The surface architecture assumes it owns the PTY lifecycle. For network-only use (your use case), the correct libghostty component is **libghostty-vt** (the zero-dependency VT parser/state library) — but that is a lower-level building block; you would still need to wire rendering yourself. The iOS SSH apps (Echo, Geistty, etc.) are presumably solving this by creating a minimal local shell or by using libghostty's surface with a custom command that bridges to the network.
- SWIFT INTEGRATION: Requires C-ABI bridging via the XCFramework (compiled Zig static library). The Lakr233 package abstracts this reasonably but it is still a binary dependency — you get no Swift source to debug, and the Zig toolchain is needed to rebuild. Not pure SPM source.
- RENDERING: Metal GPU pipeline, Ghostty-class rendering quality. Ligatures, subpixel rendering, 60/120fps. Confirmed Kitty graphics protocol support (Mitchell tweet: "Libghostty now fully exposes the Kitty Graphics Protocol").
- EMBEDDING API MATURITY: The C API header comment says "This isn't meant to be a general purpose embedding API (yet)." Mitchell's blog post (mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming) describes it as public alpha. The Lakr233/libghostty-spm wrapper adds a Swift ergonomics layer on top. The "blowing up in usage" tweet was June 2026 — meaning it works, but the API surface may still shift.
- MAINTENANCE: Ghostty is under active development. Lakr233/libghostty-spm auto-publishes weekly via a scheduled CI job tracking the latest Ghostty tag.
- LICENSE: MIT (Ghostty).
verdict.correction: The claim requires splitting into two sub-claims: (1) For briannadoubt/GhosttyKit and finnvoor/GhosttyKit — REFUTED. Both Package.swift files declare macOS(.v13) only, and the vendored XCFramework Info.plist contains only a macos-arm64 slice. (2) For Lakr233/libghostty-spm — CONFIRMED. Package.swift declares iOS(.v15) + macOS(.v13) + macCatalyst(.v15), and the CI matrix explicitly builds aarch64-ios (iphoneos) and simulator slices, publishing them in the release zip. The correct SPM dependency for iOS embedding is Lakr233/libghostty-spm, not the simpler GhosttyKit wrappers.

verifications.claim: Whether VVTerm is actually shipping production App Store builds against the XCFramework (it claims to be on the App Store; verify the App Store listing is live and the iOS target is real, not just macOS)

verdict.claim: VVTerm is actually shipping production App Store builds against the libghostty XCFramework, and its iOS target is real (not just macOS)
verdict.evidence: The App Store listing at https://apps.apple.com/app/vvterm/id6757482822 (App ID 6757482822) is live as of the research date. Developer is Vivy Technologies Co., Limited. Version 2.2 was released 4 days prior to this research. Supported platforms are explicitly iPhone (iOS 16.1+), iPad (iPadOS 16.1+), Mac (macOS 13.3+, Apple Silicon only), and Apple Vision (visionOS 1.0+). The app is 51.6 MB in the store. The VVTerm website (https://vvterm.com/) confirms libghostty as the renderer and shows a live App Store download link. The vivy-company/vvterm GitHub repo exists and documents iOS as a target. The iOS target is thus real and confirmed shipping, not just macOS.
verdict.correction: No correction needed. The claim is confirmed. The only nuance: the macOS build requires Apple Silicon (no Intel support), and the App Store rating is only 4 reviews at 5.0, indicating relatively small but live user base.
